title: What a model actually is
summary: A model is a function whose behavior is set by numbers learned from data, not written by hand.
body_html:
A machine learning model is a function. It takes an input (pixels, text, a row of numbers) and produces an output (a label, a next word, a price). What makes it learned rather than programmed is that its behavior is controlled by a large set of numbers called parameters or weights, and those numbers are fitted to data instead of chosen by a human.

Training is the process that sets the weights so the model's outputs match known examples. After training, the same fixed weights run on new inputs. Nothing inside understands in a human sense; the model has found a pattern that predicts well on data that looks like what it saw.

This reframing pays off immediately. If a model is a fitted function, its strengths and failures become predictable: it is strong where your input resembles its training data and unreliable as you move away from that. Magic has no failure model. A fitted function does.
key_points: A model is a function whose behavior is set by learned weights.
Weights come from fitting data during training, not from hand-written rules.
Output quality tracks how well the training data covers your input.
Treating it as a fitted function makes its failures predictable.

links.title: 3Blue1Brown: Neural Networks
links.note: Visual intuition for what the numbers in a model do.

links.title: Google: Machine Learning Crash Course
links.note: A short, hands-on tour of the core ideas.

flashcards.front: In one sentence, what is a model?
flashcards.back: A function whose behavior is set by weights learned from data.

flashcards.front: Where do a model's weights come from?
flashcards.back: They are fitted during training to match known examples.

flashcards.front: Why do models fail outside their training distribution?
flashcards.back: They interpolate patterns they have seen and have no rule beyond them.

quiz.q: What distinguishes a learned model from an ordinary program?
choices: Its behavior is controlled by weights fitted to data rather than hand-written rules.
It runs faster than hand-written code.
It never makes mistakes once trained.
It understands inputs the way a person does.
quiz.explain: The defining trait is learned parameters, not speed, infallibility, or understanding.

quiz.q: Why is calling a model a fitted function more useful than calling it magic?
choices: Because it sounds more technical.
Because a fitted function has a predictable failure model: it degrades away from its training data.
Because functions are always correct.
Because it means the model has no weights.
quiz.explain: The point is predictability of failure, which magic cannot give you.

exercise.prompt: A classifier was trained only on photos of golden retrievers labeled 'dog'. You show it a husky. What is the safest expectation?
choices: It may be unreliable, because the husky is outside the data it was fitted on.
It cannot output anything unless 'husky' is in the prompt.
It will refuse to answer.
It will classify the husky correctly because training lowered the loss.
exercise.explain: A model is a function fitted to its training data; performance degrades as inputs move away from that data.

outcome: You can explain what a model is and why its failures are predictable.

title: Neurons, layers, and why depth matters
summary: A network stacks weighted sums and nonlinear activations so layers can compose simple features into abstract ones.
body_html:
An artificial neuron does something small: it multiplies each input by a weight, adds them together with a bias, and passes the result through a nonlinear activation function. Put many neurons side by side and you have a layer. Stack layers and the output of one becomes the input of the next.

The nonlinearity is not a detail, it is the whole point. Without it, any stack of layers collapses algebraically into a single linear function, and a single linear function cannot bend to fit curved patterns. The activation is what lets depth add expressive power instead of just more arithmetic.

Depth buys composition. Early layers tend to capture simple structure, and later layers combine those into more abstract concepts. Deep learning is just neural networks with enough layers for that composition to pay off on hard problems like vision and language.
key_points: A neuron is a weighted sum plus a bias plus a nonlinear activation.
Activations add nonlinearity, which is what makes depth meaningful.
Without nonlinearity, a deep network reduces to one linear layer.
Deeper layers compose simple features into abstract ones.

links.title: 3Blue1Brown: Neural Networks
links.note: The clearest visual account of neurons and layers.

links.title: fast.ai: Practical Deep Learning
links.note: Train real networks top-down, then learn the internals.

flashcards.front: What three operations make up a neuron?
flashcards.back: A weighted sum of inputs, a bias, and a nonlinear activation.

flashcards.front: What does an activation function add?
flashcards.back: Nonlinearity, so stacked layers can model curved patterns.

flashcards.front: What happens to a deep network with no activations?
flashcards.back: It collapses into a single linear function.

quiz.q: Why is the activation function essential to a neural network?
choices: It replaces the need for data.
It makes training faster.
Without it, stacked layers reduce to one linear function and cannot fit curved patterns.
It stores the model's weights.
quiz.explain: Nonlinearity is what gives depth its expressive power.

quiz.q: What is the practical meaning of depth in a network?
choices: Depth removes the need for training.
Depth only affects memory usage.
More layers always means better accuracy.
Later layers can compose earlier features into more abstract representations.
quiz.explain: Depth buys hierarchical composition of features, not guaranteed accuracy.

exercise.prompt: You stack ten linear layers with NO activation function between them. What can the network represent?
choices: Nothing; it cannot run.
Any function, because it is deep.
Far more than one layer, because depth always adds power.
Only what a single linear layer can, since without nonlinearity the stack collapses.
exercise.explain: Without a nonlinearity, composed linear layers reduce algebraically to one linear function.

outcome: You can describe how layers compose features and why nonlinearity matters.

title: How a model learns: gradient descent
summary: Training measures error with a loss, then nudges every weight downhill using gradients from backpropagation.
body_html:
Training starts with a loss function that measures how wrong the model is on a batch of examples. The goal is to lower that loss. For each weight, the gradient measures how the loss changes as that weight changes, and it points in the direction that increases the loss most steeply. To reduce the loss you step each weight a small amount in the opposite direction, the negative gradient, then repeat on the next batch. That is why the method is called gradient descent.

Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes all those gradients efficiently. It runs the chain rule backward through the network, so one backward pass gives the slope of the loss with respect to every weight at once. This is what makes training large models practical.

The step size is the learning rate. Too large and training overshoots or diverges; too small and it crawls. Training is iterative and stochastic: it does not solve for a perfect answer, it descends toward a good-enough minimum over many passes through the data.
key_points: Loss measures wrongness; training tries to lower it.
A gradient points uphill (steepest loss increase); descent steps each weight the opposite way.
Backpropagation computes all gradients with one backward pass via the chain rule.
The learning rate sets step size, trading stability against speed.

links.title: Karpathy: Neural Networks Zero to Hero
links.note: Builds backpropagation from scratch, step by step.

links.title: 3Blue1Brown: Neural Networks
links.note: The gradient descent and backprop chapters are excellent.

flashcards.front: What direction does a gradient point?
flashcards.back: Toward the steepest increase in loss; descent moves each weight the opposite way (the negative gradient).

flashcards.front: What algorithm computes gradients efficiently?
flashcards.back: Backpropagation, the chain rule run backward through the network.

flashcards.front: What does the learning rate control?
flashcards.back: The step size, and so the tradeoff between stability and speed.

quiz.q: What is backpropagation responsible for?
choices: Computing the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight.
Collecting the training data.
Serving the model in production.
Choosing the model architecture.
quiz.explain: Backprop computes gradients; everything else is a separate concern.

quiz.q: A model's loss keeps bouncing up and down and never settles. What is a likely cause?
choices: The data is too clean.
The learning rate is too high, so steps overshoot the minimum.
The model has too few weights.
Backpropagation is disabled.
quiz.explain: An oversized learning rate causes the descent to overshoot and oscillate.

exercise.prompt: Put one step of gradient-descent training in the order it happens.
items: Run the batch forward to get predictions
Compute the loss against the targets
Backpropagate to get the gradient for every weight
Step each weight a little along the negative gradient
exercise.explain: Forward, loss, backprop, update, then repeat over many batches. The learning rate sets how big each update step is.

outcome: You can explain how a model learns from data and what the learning rate does.

title: Overfitting and generalization
summary: Low training loss is a proxy; the real target is low loss on data the model has never seen.
body_html:
It is easy to make training loss small. A model with enough capacity can memorize its training set, quirks and noise included. That is overfitting: the model learns the specifics of the training data instead of the pattern that generalizes, so it looks great in training and fails in deployment.

You detect overfitting by holding out a validation set the model never trains on and watching the gap between training and validation loss. When training loss keeps dropping while validation loss flattens or rises, the model has started memorizing. The mirror-image failure is underfitting, where the model is too weak to capture the pattern at all.

The fixes trade raw capacity for generalization: more and more varied data, regularization such as weight decay and dropout, early stopping when validation loss turns, and simpler models when data is scarce. Good training lives in the band between underfitting and overfitting.
key_points: Training loss is a proxy; held-out loss is the real target.
Overfitting is learning noise and specifics instead of the general pattern.
Detect it with a validation set and the train-versus-validation gap.
More data, regularization, and early stopping push toward generalization.

links.title: An Introduction to Statistical Learning
links.note: The classic free treatment of bias, variance, and generalization.

links.title: Google: Machine Learning Crash Course
links.note: Clear sections on generalization and regularization.

flashcards.front: What is overfitting?
flashcards.back: Fitting training noise and specifics, so performance on new data drops.

flashcards.front: How do you detect overfitting?
flashcards.back: Watch the gap between training loss and held-out validation loss.

flashcards.front: Name two ways to reduce overfitting.
flashcards.back: More varied data, and regularization like weight decay, dropout, or early stopping.

quiz.q: Training loss is near zero but the model performs poorly on new inputs. What is happening?
choices: The model has too many layers to train.
Underfitting.
Overfitting: the model memorized the training set instead of generalizing.
The learning rate is too small.
quiz.explain: Low train loss with poor new-data performance is the signature of overfitting.

quiz.q: Why hold out a validation set?
choices: To increase the model's capacity.
Because backpropagation requires it.
To make training faster.
To estimate performance on data the model did not train on.
quiz.explain: Validation data approximates the deployment distribution the model has not seen.

exercise.prompt: During training, training loss keeps falling but validation loss has started rising. What should you conclude?
choices: The model is starting to overfit; consider early stopping or regularization.
The learning rate is too low.
The validation set is unnecessary.
The model is underfitting and needs more capacity.
exercise.explain: A falling train loss with a rising validation loss is the canonical overfitting signal.

outcome: You can diagnose overfitting versus underfitting and name fixes for each.

title: Tokens and embeddings
summary: Language models read text as tokens and represent meaning as vectors called embeddings.
body_html:
Most language models do not read raw characters or whole words. Text is first split into tokens, which are common chunks of text such as whole words, word fragments, or punctuation. A few models work at the byte or character level instead, but token-based models are the norm. This is why model limits and pricing are quoted in tokens, and why a rare word may cost several tokens while a common one costs one.

Each token is mapped to a vector of numbers called an embedding. The model learns these vectors so that tokens used in similar ways land near each other in the vector space. Geometry then carries useful structure: distances and directions between embeddings often track relationships, though the mapping is not perfectly semantic, and modern models use context-dependent representations rather than one fixed vector per word.

Embeddings are not unique to language. The same idea represents images, audio, and users as vectors, which is what powers semantic search and recommendation. The core move is always the same: turn discrete things into vectors so a model can do math on meaning.
key_points: Models read text as tokens, not raw characters or words.
Token count drives context limits and cost.
An embedding is a learned vector representing a token or item.
Similar items land near each other, so geometry encodes meaning.

links.title: The Illustrated Word2vec
links.note: The clearest visual intro to embeddings and vector meaning.

links.title: Hugging Face: NLP Course (Tokenizers)
links.note: How tokenization actually works in practice.

flashcards.front: What is a token?
flashcards.back: A common chunk of text (word, fragment, or punctuation) that the model reads as one unit.

flashcards.front: What is an embedding?
flashcards.back: A learned vector that represents a token or item so similar ones are nearby.

flashcards.front: Why are model limits quoted in tokens, not words?
flashcards.back: Because the model processes tokens, and words map to a variable number of them.

quiz.q: Why might a long, rare word cost more tokens than a short common one?
choices: Rare or long words are split into several subword tokens.
Long words are always one token.
The model spells out each letter as a token.
Token count depends only on language, not the word.
quiz.explain: Tokenizers break uncommon words into multiple subword pieces.

quiz.q: What property makes embeddings useful for semantic search?
choices: They compress text to save disk space.
Similar meanings map to nearby vectors, so closeness approximates relatedness.
They convert text to lowercase.
They remove all punctuation.
quiz.explain: Search ranks by vector distance, which tracks semantic similarity.

exercise.prompt: Each dot is a word, placed so similar meanings sit close together. Click a word to see its three nearest neighbors.
exercise.note: king, queen, man, woman form a parallelogram: king minus man plus woman lands exactly on queen here. That analogy is a real property of learned embeddings.

outcome: You can explain tokens, why cost scales with them, and what embeddings encode.

title: Attention and the transformer
summary: Attention lets each token gather information from the most relevant other tokens, and the transformer stacks that idea.
body_html:
The transformer is the architecture behind modern language models. Its core mechanism is self-attention: for each token, the model computes how much the other tokens it can see should influence it, then mixes their information accordingly. A pronoun can look back to the noun it refers to; a verb can attend to its subject. The model learns what to attend to from data.

Attention works through three learned projections per token, called query, key, and value. A token's query is compared against every token's key to produce attention weights, and those weights combine the values. Because tokens are processed in parallel rather than strictly one after another, transformers train far more efficiently than the strictly sequential models before them. Self-attention still has a real cost: standard attention grows roughly with the square of the sequence length, which is why very long context is expensive. Methods like FlashAttention cut the memory cost, and newer architectures reduce it further, but the quadratic baseline is the thing to remember. One detail matters for generation: most current chat LLMs are decoder-only and apply a causal mask, so each token attends only to itself and the tokens before it, never to future ones.

A transformer stacks many attention layers with small feed-forward networks between them. Depth lets later layers build on the relationships found by earlier ones. This single design, scaled up and trained on enormous text corpora, is what produced today's large language models.
key_points: Self-attention lets each token pull information from the most relevant tokens.
It uses learned query, key, and value projections per token.
All tokens attend in parallel, which makes training on long sequences efficient.
A transformer stacks many attention layers; scaling this gave us modern LLMs.

links.title: The Illustrated Transformer
links.note: The visual gold standard for understanding attention.

links.title: LLM Visualization
links.note: Walk through a transformer generating text in 3D.

links.title: Attention Is All You Need
links.note: The original transformer paper.

flashcards.front: What does self-attention compute?
flashcards.back: How much each token should be influenced by every other token, then mixes them.

flashcards.front: What are the three projections in attention?
flashcards.back: Query, key, and value.

flashcards.front: Why do transformers train efficiently on long text?
flashcards.back: All tokens attend in parallel instead of being processed strictly in sequence.

quiz.q: What problem does self-attention solve for language?
choices: It guarantees the output is factually correct.
It removes the need for training data.
It lets each token draw on the most relevant other tokens, capturing long-range relationships.
It compresses the model to fewer weights.
quiz.explain: Attention routes information between related tokens regardless of distance.

quiz.q: In attention, what is compared to produce the weights?
choices: Each token's value against the loss.
The learning rate against the batch size.
Two random tokens chosen each step.
Each token's query against every token's key.
quiz.explain: Query-key comparisons produce the attention weights that combine the values.

exercise.prompt: While a decoder-only LLM generates text, can the token at position 5 attend to the token at position 9?
choices: Only in the first layer.
Yes, every token attends to every other token.
No, causal masking blocks attending to later tokens.
Only if temperature is high.
exercise.explain: Text-generating LLMs use a causal mask, so a token attends only to itself and earlier tokens.

outcome: You can explain attention and why the transformer enabled modern LLMs.

title: Pretraining, fine-tuning, and RLHF
summary: Modern models are built in stages: broad pretraining, targeted fine-tuning, then preference tuning to shape behavior.
body_html:
A frontier model is not trained once. It is built in stages. Pretraining is the expensive first stage: the model learns to predict the next token across a vast text corpus, which forces it to absorb grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, and style. The result is a capable but raw model that completes text rather than following instructions.

Fine-tuning adapts that base model to a narrower goal using a smaller, curated dataset. Instruction tuning, where the model is trained on many examples of requests and good responses, is what turns a text completer into something that follows directions. Fine-tuning is far cheaper than pretraining because it adjusts an already-capable model.

Preference tuning is a major shaping stage. In reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), people rank candidate responses, a reward model learns those preferences, and the model is optimized to produce responses people prefer. Related methods like direct preference optimization reach a similar goal without a separate reinforcement learning loop. This shapes tone, helpfulness, and safety. Real pipelines are rarely a single pass: they often layer several rounds of fine-tuning, safety tuning, distillation, and tool-use training rather than one neat final step.
key_points: Pretraining teaches broad capability via next-token prediction on huge corpora.
Fine-tuning cheaply adapts the base model; instruction tuning makes it follow directions.
RLHF and related methods optimize toward human-preferred responses.
Behavior like tone, helpfulness, and safety is shaped in the preference stage.

links.title: Hugging Face: Alignment Handbook
links.note: Recipes and code for instruction tuning and preference optimization.

links.title: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
links.note: Preference tuning without a separate reinforcement learning loop.

flashcards.front: What does pretraining teach a model?
flashcards.back: Broad capability by predicting the next token across a vast corpus.

flashcards.front: What does instruction tuning change?
flashcards.back: It turns a raw text completer into a model that follows directions.

flashcards.front: What is the goal of RLHF?
flashcards.back: To optimize the model toward responses humans prefer.

quiz.q: Why is fine-tuning much cheaper than pretraining?
choices: It adjusts an already-capable model on a smaller, targeted dataset.
It skips backpropagation.
It does not change any weights.
It uses a larger dataset than pretraining.
quiz.explain: Fine-tuning starts from a trained base, so it needs far less data and compute.

quiz.q: What does the preference-tuning stage primarily shape?
choices: The model's raw factual knowledge.
Tone, helpfulness, and safety, by optimizing toward preferred responses.
The tokenizer.
The size of the context window.
quiz.explain: RLHF and similar methods primarily shape behavior and preferences; base capability comes from pretraining, though post-training can also lift measured performance.

exercise.prompt: Put the stages of building a modern model in order.
items: Pretrain on a vast corpus by predicting the next token
Tune toward human-preferred responses (RLHF or similar)
exercise.explain: Broad pretraining first, then instruction tuning to follow directions, then preference tuning to shape behavior. Real pipelines layer more rounds, but this is the backbone.

outcome: You can describe the pretraining, fine-tuning, and preference-tuning pipeline.

title: Inference, sampling, and temperature
summary: At generation time a model outputs a probability over next tokens, and sampling settings decide how that distribution becomes text.
body_html:
When a language model generates text, it does not emit a single answer. At each step it produces a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary of next tokens. Generation is the loop of sampling one token from that distribution, appending it, and predicting the next. This is why the same prompt can give different outputs.

Temperature controls how sharp or flat that distribution is before sampling. Low temperature concentrates probability on the top tokens, giving focused and repetitive output. High temperature flattens the distribution, giving more varied and creative but riskier output. Temperature near zero is effectively greedy: always take the most likely token.

Other controls shape the candidate pool. Top-k keeps only the k most likely tokens; top-p, or nucleus sampling, keeps the smallest set whose probability adds up to p. Picking these settings is a deliberate tradeoff between reliability and diversity, chosen to fit the task rather than left to chance.
key_points: A model outputs a probability distribution over next tokens at each step.
Sampling from that distribution is why outputs vary across runs.
Temperature sharpens or flattens the distribution: low is focused, high is diverse.
Top-k and top-p limit the candidate pool to trade reliability against diversity.

links.title: LLM Visualization
links.note: See the next-token distribution and sampling in action.

links.title: The Illustrated Transformer
links.note: Background on how the output distribution is produced.

flashcards.front: What does a model output at each generation step?
flashcards.back: A probability distribution over all possible next tokens.

flashcards.front: What does temperature do?
flashcards.back: Sharpens (low) or flattens (high) the distribution before sampling.

flashcards.front: What does top-p (nucleus) sampling keep?
flashcards.back: The smallest set of top tokens whose probabilities sum to p.

quiz.q: Why does the same prompt sometimes produce different answers?
choices: The model retrains on every request.
The model's weights change between runs.
Each token is sampled from a probability distribution, so runs can differ.
The prompt is secretly edited.
quiz.explain: Sampling introduces variation unless you force greedy decoding.

quiz.q: You need maximally consistent, repeatable output. What setting helps most?
choices: A larger vocabulary.
More layers.
A very high temperature.
A very low temperature (near zero), approaching greedy decoding.
quiz.explain: Low temperature concentrates probability on the top token, reducing variation.

exercise.prompt: Drag the temperature. Low values sharpen the distribution onto the top token; high values flatten it toward uniform.
exercise.question: Set the temperature near 0.2, then near 1.8, and watch how the top token's share changes.
exercise.explain: Each probability is softmax(logit / temperature). Near zero the top logit dominates (greedy); high temperature spreads probability toward uniform.

outcome: You can choose sampling settings to match a task's need for reliability or diversity.

title: The context window
summary: The context window is the model's working memory: the total tokens it can attend to at once, and everything outside it is gone.
body_html:
The context window is the maximum number of tokens a model can consider at one time, counting both your input and its own output. It is the model's working memory for a single request. Anything that does not fit, or that scrolls past the limit in a long conversation, is simply not available to the model.

This explains a lot of real behavior. A model forgetting something you said earlier in a long chat usually means it fell outside the window, not that the model chose to ignore it. The window is also why long documents must be chunked, and why retrieval systems fetch only the most relevant passages to spend the budget well.

Bigger windows help but are not free. Attention cost grows with sequence length, and a model given a huge context still attends unevenly, often handling the start and end of a long input better than the middle. Treat context as a scarce budget: put what matters most where the model will use it.
key_points: The context window is the total tokens (input plus output) a model can attend to at once.
Anything outside the window is unavailable; that is why long chats forget.
Long inputs are chunked and retrieval fetches only the most relevant parts.
Larger windows cost more and are used unevenly, so treat context as a budget.

links.title: Hugging Face: NLP Course
links.note: Background on how models process input sequences.

links.title: LLM Visualization
links.note: See how input tokens flow through the model at once.

flashcards.front: What is the context window?
flashcards.back: The maximum tokens, input plus output, a model can attend to in one request.

flashcards.front: Why does a model forget earlier parts of a long chat?
flashcards.back: Those tokens scrolled outside the context window and are no longer available.

flashcards.front: Why treat context as a budget?
flashcards.back: It is finite, costs grow with length, and models use long context unevenly.

quiz.q: In a long conversation, the model stops referencing something you said at the start. Most likely cause?
choices: The early tokens fell outside the context window.
The temperature was too low.
The model needs retraining.
The model deleted the information on purpose.
quiz.explain: Once content scrolls past the window, the model cannot see it.

quiz.q: Why do retrieval systems fetch only the most relevant passages instead of the whole document?
choices: To hide information from the model.
Because context is a finite, costly budget that should hold what matters most.
Because models cannot read documents at all.
To slow the model down.
quiz.explain: Spending the limited window on the most relevant content gives better answers per token.

exercise.prompt: You are assembling a prompt for an 8,000-token context window. Drag the reservations and watch what fits. Output is reserved first, then the system prompt; retrieved context and chat history are truncated to fit, history first.
exercise.question: Push retrieved context and history up until you go over 8,000. Which gets truncated first, and why?
exercise.explain: Everything shares one fixed budget. Output and system are protected, so when the total exceeds the window the lowest-priority context (oldest chat history) is dropped first, then retrieved context. Nothing outside the window is visible to the model.

outcome: You can explain the context window and why it governs memory, chunking, and retrieval.

title: Data quality and leakage
summary: A model is only as good as its data; quality, coverage, and especially leakage matter more than clever architecture.
body_html:
It is tempting to focus on the model, but data usually decides the outcome. Noisy or inconsistent labels, duplicates, and biased coverage cap performance no matter how good the architecture is. The unglamorous work of cleaning, deduplicating, and balancing data often beats a fancier model on the same data.

The silent killer is data leakage: information from the test set, or from the future, sneaking into training. When it happens, evaluation scores look excellent and then collapse in production, because the model was effectively graded on data it had already seen. Leakage hides in subtle places: the same user or record in both train and test, a feature that encodes the label, or a random split of time-ordered data.

The defenses are concrete. Deduplicate between train and test, and split by time or by entity when the real task is to generalize to new periods or new users, not a random row. Check that labels are consistent and that the data covers the inputs you will actually see, because a model is unreliable wherever its training data was thin.
key_points: Data quality and coverage often matter more than model architecture.
Leakage is information from the test set or the future entering training; it inflates scores then collapses in production.
Dedup train and test, and split by time or entity when that matches the real task.
A model is unreliable on input regions its training data underrepresents.

links.title: An Introduction to Statistical Learning
links.note: Foundations of train/test discipline and generalization.

links.title: Google: Machine Learning Crash Course
links.note: Practical guidance on data, splits, and fairness.

flashcards.front: What often matters more than model architecture?
flashcards.back: Data quality, coverage, and the absence of leakage.

flashcards.front: What is data leakage?
flashcards.back: Information from the test set or the future entering training, inflating scores that then collapse in production.

flashcards.front: When should you split by time or entity instead of randomly?
flashcards.back: When the real task is to generalize to new periods or new users, not a random row.

quiz.q: A model scores 99% in testing but fails in production. You find the same customers in both train and test sets. What happened?
choices: The test set was too large.
The model is too small.
Data leakage: overlapping train and test inflated the score.
The temperature was wrong.
quiz.explain: Shared records across splits let the model be graded on data it trained on; split by entity.

quiz.q: Why split time-ordered data by time rather than randomly?
choices: To use less data.
Because random splits are illegal.
To make training faster.
Because the real task is predicting the future, and a random split leaks future information.
quiz.explain: A random split lets future rows train the model, leaking information it would not have in production.

exercise.prompt: A churn model includes a feature 'account_closed_date'. It scores almost perfectly in testing. What should you suspect?
choices: The model is simply excellent.
Target leakage: the feature encodes the outcome you are predicting.
The learning rate is too high.
The data set is too small.
exercise.explain: A feature that is only known after the outcome leaks the label; near-perfect scores are a red flag, not a triumph.

outcome: You can spot data-quality problems and leakage that inflate scores before deployment.

title: Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning
summary: The three classic learning settings differ by what signal the model learns from: labels, structure, or reward.
body_html:
Before language models, machine learning was usually sorted into three settings, and the split still organizes the field. Supervised learning trains on labeled examples: each input comes with the correct output, and the model learns to map one to the other. Classification (spam or not) and regression (predict a price) are supervised. Most of the accuracy metrics you have seen assume this setting, because you need ground-truth labels to score against.

Unsupervised learning has no labels. The model finds structure in the data itself: clustering groups similar items, dimensionality reduction compresses them, and density models learn what typical data looks like. You reach for it when labels are expensive or you do not yet know what you are looking for. Notably, the pretraining of large language models is self-supervised, a close cousin: the label is just the next token, generated for free from the text.

Reinforcement learning is different again: an agent takes actions, receives a reward signal, and learns a policy that maximizes reward over time. It fits sequential decision problems where there is no single correct answer, only outcomes. RLHF, which you met earlier, borrows this machinery to tune models toward preferred behavior. Knowing which setting you are in tells you what data you need and how you can even measure success.
key_points: Supervised learning maps inputs to known labels (classification, regression).
Unsupervised learning finds structure without labels (clustering, dimensionality reduction).
LLM pretraining is self-supervised: the next token is a free label.
Reinforcement learning optimizes a policy from a reward signal over time.

links.title: An Introduction to Statistical Learning
links.note: The classic treatment of supervised and unsupervised methods.

links.title: Google: Machine Learning Crash Course (Classification)
links.note: Hands-on intro to supervised classification and its metrics.

flashcards.front: What does supervised learning require?
flashcards.back: Labeled examples: each input paired with its correct output.

flashcards.front: What does unsupervised learning find?
flashcards.back: Structure in unlabeled data, like clusters or a low-dimensional representation.

flashcards.front: Why is LLM pretraining called self-supervised?
flashcards.back: The label is the next token, generated for free from the text itself.

quiz.q: You have a pile of unlabeled support tickets and want to group similar ones. Which setting fits?
choices: Unsupervised clustering.
Reinforcement learning.
None; it cannot be done.
Supervised classification.
quiz.explain: No labels and a grouping goal points to unsupervised clustering.

quiz.q: Why can you compute accuracy for a supervised model but not directly for plain clustering?
choices: Clustering is slower.
Accuracy needs ground-truth labels, which unsupervised data lacks.
Clustering has no inputs.
Accuracy only works on text.
quiz.explain: Accuracy compares predictions to known labels; unsupervised settings have none.

exercise.prompt: You want a model that learns to play a game well by trying moves and seeing the score. Which learning setting is this?
choices: Reinforcement learning.
Self-supervised pretraining.
Supervised learning.
Unsupervised learning.
exercise.explain: Acting, receiving a reward, and improving a policy over time is reinforcement learning.

outcome: You can classify a problem as supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement learning and say what data each needs.

title: Inside a transformer block: residuals, normalization, positions
summary: Attention gets the headlines, but three quieter pieces make deep transformers trainable: residual connections, normalization, and positional encoding.
body_html:
A transformer is a stack of identical blocks, and each block has two sublayers: multi-head self-attention, then a small position-wise feedforward network. Stacking dozens of these would normally be impossible to train, because gradients vanish or explode on the way down. Residual connections fix that: each sublayer computes an update and adds it to its input rather than replacing it, so the block learns a correction to the running representation. The unbroken add-path gives gradients a clean route back through every layer, which is what lets the network go deep.

Normalization keeps the numbers in a sane range. Layer normalization rescales each token's vector to a stable mean and variance before (or after) each sublayer, so one runaway feature does not dominate and training stays steady across the stack. The exact placement, pre-norm versus post-norm, changes stability and is a real architecture choice, but the purpose is the same: keep activations well-conditioned so the residual updates compose cleanly.

Attention itself is order-blind. Self-attention is permutation-equivariant: permute the input tokens and its outputs just permute the same way, because each position is a weighted sum over the same set of tokens. So raw attention carries no information about order. Positional encoding injects order back in: as fixed sinusoids or learned vectors added to the embeddings, or, in most current models, as a relative or rotary scheme applied inside attention. Without it, the model has no way to tell 'dog bites man' from 'man bites dog'. Together, residuals make depth trainable, normalization keeps it stable, and positions give attention something to be ordered about.
key_points: A block is self-attention plus a feedforward sublayer, each wrapped in a residual add and a normalization.
Residual connections add each sublayer's output to its input, giving gradients a clean path through a deep stack.
Normalization (layer norm) keeps activations well-conditioned so training stays stable.
Attention is order-blind; positional encoding (sinusoidal, learned, or rotary) restores token order.

links.title: The Illustrated Transformer
links.note: The clearest visual walkthrough of a transformer block.

links.title: Attention Is All You Need
links.note: The original architecture: residuals, layer norm, and sinusoidal positions.

flashcards.front: What problem do residual connections solve in deep transformers?
flashcards.back: Vanishing/exploding gradients. Adding each sublayer's output to its input gives gradients an unbroken path through the whole stack, making depth trainable.

flashcards.front: Why does a transformer need positional encoding?
flashcards.back: Raw self-attention is order-blind (a weighted sum over a set). Positional encoding injects token order so word order carries meaning.

flashcards.front: What does layer normalization do?
flashcards.back: Rescales each token vector to a stable mean and variance so no single feature dominates and training stays steady across layers.

quiz.q: Remove the residual connections from a 48-layer transformer. The most likely result is:
choices: It can no longer attend to any token
Faster training
It trains poorly because gradients struggle to reach early layers
Identical behavior
quiz.explain: Residual add-paths are what let gradients flow through a deep stack. Remove them and early layers barely learn.

quiz.q: Self-attention with the positional information removed would treat 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' as:
choices: Grammatically invalid
Two different token counts
Opposite in meaning
The same, because without positional information self-attention carries no order information
quiz.explain: Without positions, self-attention is permutation-equivariant (permuting the inputs just permutes the outputs), so no information distinguishes word order.

quiz.q: The position-wise feedforward sublayer in a block:
choices: Applies the same small network to each token's vector independently
Replaces attention
Stores the positional encodings
Mixes information across tokens
quiz.explain: Attention mixes across tokens; the feedforward sublayer transforms each position independently with shared weights.

exercise.prompt: Self-attention lets each word pull information from the others. Pick a query word and watch how strongly it attends to each token.

outcome: Explain what residuals, normalization, and positional encoding each contribute, and trace data through one transformer block.

sources.claim: Residual connections and layer normalization wrap each sublayer in the original transformer.
sources.title: Vaswani et al., Attention Is All You Need (2017)

title: How byte-pair encoding builds a vocabulary
summary: Tokenizers do not split on words. Byte-pair encoding learns a vocabulary of frequent fragments, which is why token counts and costs surprise people.
body_html:
A model cannot read raw text; it reads token ids. The tokenizer that produces them is trained separately, before the model, and most current models use byte-pair encoding (BPE) or a close variant. BPE starts from the smallest units (bytes or characters) and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new symbol. Run that thousands of times and you get a vocabulary where common words like 'the' are a single token, while rare or novel strings stay split into several pieces. The merges are frozen into the vocabulary; at inference the tokenizer just applies them greedily.

This explains the counts that catch people out. A short common English word is often one token; a long rare technical term, a misspelling, or a string of digits can be many tokens, because no frequent merges cover them. Whitespace and capitalization matter too, since ' the' and 'the' may be different tokens. Code, non-English scripts, and emoji often cost more tokens per visible character than plain English, which is exactly the text BPE vocabularies are usually trained on.

Two practical consequences follow. First, your context window and your bill are denominated in tokens, not words or characters, so the same screenful of text can cost very differently depending on what it contains. Second, tokenization is a real source of model quirks: arithmetic on multi-digit numbers and exact character manipulation are harder partly because digits and characters get grouped in ways that hide their structure from the model.
key_points: BPE learns a vocabulary by repeatedly merging the most frequent adjacent unit pair, starting from bytes or characters.
Common words become single tokens; rare strings, digits, and other scripts split into many.
Whitespace and case can change which token a string maps to.
Token count, not word or character count, drives context limits and cost.

links.title: Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units (BPE)
links.note: The paper that brought byte-pair encoding to NLP.

links.title: Hugging Face: Summary of the tokenizers
links.note: BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram compared with worked examples.

flashcards.front: In one sentence, how does BPE build its vocabulary?
flashcards.back: It starts from bytes or characters and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token, thousands of times.

flashcards.front: Why does a long rare technical term cost many tokens?
flashcards.back: No frequent merges cover it, so it stays split into several subword pieces, while common words collapse to one token.

flashcards.front: Why are context limits and bills counted in tokens, not words?
flashcards.back: The model only sees tokens. The same visible text can be many or few tokens depending on how BPE fragments it.

quiz.q: Which input is likely to use the MOST tokens for its visible length?
choices: The common word 'the'
A long, rare chemical name the tokenizer has never merged
A single space
The word 'and'
quiz.explain: Common words are single tokens; a rare unmerged string fragments into many subword pieces, so it costs the most tokens per character.

quiz.q: Why can a model struggle with multi-digit arithmetic partly because of tokenization?
choices: Tokenizers round numbers
Digits are never tokenized
Digit groups can be merged into tokens that hide individual place values from the model
Numbers use a separate model
quiz.explain: BPE may group digits into chunks, obscuring the per-digit structure arithmetic needs. It is one contributor to number-handling quirks.

quiz.q: ' the' (with a leading space) and 'the' in a typical BPE tokenizer are:
choices: Never tokenizable
Merged into one character
Always the same token
Potentially different tokens, because whitespace is part of the unit
quiz.explain: Many BPE vocabularies treat leading whitespace as part of the token, so the spaced and unspaced forms can differ.

exercise.prompt: Type any text and watch it split into tokens. Try a rare long word, ALL CAPS, code, or heavy punctuation, and switch granularity to see the token count change.
exercise.text: Tokenization isn't always intuitive: pneumonoultramicroscopicsilicovolcanoconiosis fragments into many pieces.

outcome: Predict roughly how text fragments into tokens and explain why count and cost are denominated in tokens.

title: Capstone: build and judge a tiny classifier
summary: Tie the foundations together. Walk a small classifier from data split to a go/no-go decision, reading a confusion matrix instead of trusting a single accuracy number.
body_html:
You have built up the pieces: data, training, loss and overfitting, metrics. This capstone runs them on one concrete task. You are building a classifier that flags support tickets as urgent or not, so urgent ones jump the queue. You have 2,000 labeled tickets. The questions below are the decisions that separate a model you can trust from a demo that quietly overfits.

Work the steps in order; the reasoning is revealed after each choice. The goal is the habit of thought: split honestly, watch the dev/test gap, read the matrix to the cost that matters, and decide.
key_points: Split data into train, dev, and test; the test set is touched once, at the end.
Tuning on the test set leaks information and inflates your reported score.
Read the confusion matrix against real costs (a missed urgent ticket vs a false alarm).
A go decision needs the metric that matches the cost, not the headline accuracy.

links.title: Google ML Crash Course: Classification
links.note: Splits, thresholds, and confusion-matrix metrics, hands-on.

flashcards.front: What is the dev (validation) set for?
flashcards.back: Tuning choices like the threshold and hyperparameters, so the test set stays untouched for one honest final estimate.

flashcards.front: Why is a high accuracy not enough to ship the urgent-ticket classifier?
flashcards.back: On imbalanced data accuracy hides misses. You need the recall on urgent tickets, since a missed urgent ticket is the costly error.

quiz.q: You tune the decision threshold to maximize score on the test set. The problem is:
choices: You have leaked the test set into tuning, so its score no longer estimates real-world performance
The threshold cannot be tuned
Accuracy is the only valid metric
Nothing; that is correct
quiz.explain: Tuning on test contaminates it. Tune on dev; report the test score once.

quiz.q: For urgent-ticket routing, the most costly error is usually:
choices: A false alarm (non-urgent flagged urgent)
A miss (a real urgent ticket not flagged), so recall on urgent matters most
Too many true negatives
A balanced dataset
quiz.explain: Missing a genuinely urgent ticket is the expensive failure, so recall on the urgent class is the metric to defend.

exercise.prompt: Take the urgent-ticket classifier from split to decision, one step at a time.
exercise.scenario_html:
Task: classify support tickets as urgent (positive) or not. 2,000 labeled tickets; about 15% are urgent. After training and tuning on the dev set, your held-out test confusion matrix is:

TP = 78   FN = 12   FP = 60   TN = 450 (90 urgent, 510 not, in the test set).

steps.q: How should the 2,000 tickets be split?
choices: Into train, dev, and test, where test is used once at the very end
Train and test only, tuning on test
Randomly relabel and retrain
All 2,000 for training
steps.explain: Three-way split: train to fit, dev to tune the threshold and choices, test for a single honest final estimate.

steps.q: From the matrix, what is the recall (catch rate) on urgent tickets?
steps.explain: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = 78/(78+12) = 78/90 = 0.87. You catch about 87% of urgent tickets.

steps.q: Precision is 78/(78+60) = about 0.57. Given the costs, the reasonable read is:
choices: Accuracy is 88%, so nothing else matters
Reject the model; precision is too low
Recall on urgent is solid; the false alarms are tolerable since a flagged non-urgent ticket just gets a quick human glance
Ship it as the sole decision-maker with no human review
steps.explain: Missing urgent tickets is the costly error, and recall is good. False alarms cost a cheap human glance, an acceptable trade for queue routing.

steps.q: What is the honest go/no-go decision?
choices: Report only the 88% accuracy and roll out everywhere
Retrain until accuracy hits 99%
Ship as a fully automated final decision
Pilot it as a triage aid with a human reviewing flags, report recall with a confidence interval, and monitor the miss rate
steps.explain: Go, but as an aid with human review and monitoring, reporting recall with uncertainty. That respects both the value and the cost of a miss.

exercise.outcome: You split honestly, read recall and precision off a real confusion matrix, weighed them against costs, and made a defensible, monitored go decision.

outcome: Carry a classifier from an honest data split to a cost-aware, monitored decision using a confusion matrix.
